generated_by: scheduled-task workday-t10-marketing-subject-lines — assistant fallback (marketing-skills bridge unavailable from daemon shell: no ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, no plugin root on disk)

input.intro: I run a build studio called Day14 out of Southwest Florida. The premise is simple: a full business platform — marketing site, customer portal, billing, admin app, AI chatbot — shipped in 14 calendar days, fixed price, you own the code. This newsletter is the thing I wish I'd been reading three years ago when I was getting quoted $40k and 6 months by agencies. Issue #1 argues that scoping — not building — is what kills agency projects.
input.audience: SWFL operators (and adjacent small-business owners) who have been quoted by agencies, are skeptical of SaaS lock-in, and want a fast fixed-price productized build. Secondary: indie-hacker / one-operator readers who care about productized services.
input.voice: Operator, anti-jargon, anti-agency, anti-SaaS. Specific numbers (14 days, fixed price). No exclamation points. No 'we' (it's Jack, not a team). No 'transform your business'. Signed '— Jack / Day14'.

variants.subject: Scoping is what kills agency projects
variants.rationale: Lifts the issue's strongest line verbatim. Provocative, specific, and reframes the whole agency-vs-productized debate in one sentence — high open-rate hook for the target audience.

variants.subject: Why I quote 14 days, fixed price — and ship
variants.rationale: Leads with the two pillars (timeline + price model) and ends on the proof verb ('ship'). Reads like an operator note, not a marketer's hook.

variants.subject: $40k, 6 months, nothing built. There's a better way.
variants.rationale: Pulls the specific agency quote from the intro — the number is the bait. The 'better way' tail is intentionally plain so it doesn't tip into infomercial.

variants.subject: Shipping vs scoping — the whole job
variants.rationale: Distills the issue title into a tighter line and ends on a claim that earns the open. Short enough for mobile preview without truncation.

variants.subject: What one operator can ship in two weeks
variants.rationale: Anchored on the unique-positioning fact (one operator, fixed window). Reads as a curiosity hook for readers who are tired of agency-team pitches.
